items.q: What reads the mRNA codons during translation?
items.correct: tRNA molecules carrying specific amino acids — their anticodons pair with mRNA codons at the ribosome.
items.misconception: DNA directly reads the codons on mRNA and adds amino acids.
items.random_wrong: Ribosomes synthesise amino acids from scratch using the mRNA as a template.
items.why: Students miss the role of tRNA as the adaptor; they think mRNA is read by a direct chemical process rather than via tRNA matching.

items.q: What makes the antigen-binding site of an antibody specific to one antigen?
items.correct: The variable region — its amino acid sequence (and thus shape) differs between antibodies, complementing a specific antigen.
items.misconception: All antibodies have the same binding site; specificity comes from where they go in the body.
items.random_wrong: Antibodies bind to any antigen they meet first.
items.why: Students miss the role of the variable region in determining specificity, thinking antibodies are interchangeable generic 'germ-killers'.

items.q: In osmosis, which direction does water move?
items.correct: From a region of higher water potential (less concentrated solution) to lower water potential.
items.misconception: Water moves from high solute concentration to low solute concentration.
items.random_wrong: Water moves towards the sugar because sugar attracts water molecules chemically.
items.why: Students get confused between solute concentration and water potential; they flip the direction because 'high concentration' feels like it should attract.

items.q: Which bond joins a fatty acid to glycerol in a triglyceride?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Ester bond. “Peptide bond” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which level of protein structure refers to multiple polypeptide chains joined together?
items.why: Correct answer: Quaternary. “Primary” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many water molecules are released when one triglyceride forms from glycerol and 3 fatty acids?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
